title: Rootless Podman with `zfs` Storage Driver -- "Error: cannot find root filesystem"
body:
### Issue Description

Rootless Podman is unable to list ZFS filesystems.

Here is the rootless `storage.conf` file:
```
[storage]
driver = "zfs"

[storage.options.zfs]
fsname = "zroot/containers"
mountopt = "nodev"
```

This is the error I get when attempting to do anything with rootless Podman:
```
$ podman system info
Error: cannot find root filesystem zroot/containers: exit status 1: "/usr/bin/zfs list -rHp -t filesystem -o name,origin,used,available,mountpoint,compression,type,volsize,quota,referenced,written,logicalused,usedbydataset zroot/containers" => cannot open 'zroot/containers': dataset does not exist
```

When I run the command manually using the same user, I am able to list the filesystem just fine:
```
$ /usr/bin/zfs list -rHp -t filesystem -o name,origin,used,available,mountpoint,compression,type,volsize,quota,referenced,written,logicalused,usedbydataset zroot/containers
zroot/containers        -       290816  480957955968    /home/<user>/.local/share/containers  lz4     filesystem      -       0       290816  290816  137728  290816
```

`~/.local/share/containers` is owned by `<user>:<user>` and here are the ZFS permissions:
```
$ zfs allow zroot/containers
---- Permissions on zroot/containers ---------------------------------
Local+Descendent permissions:
        user <user> create,destroy,mount,snapshot
```

### Steps to reproduce the issue

1. `$ podman system reset`
2. `$ su -`
3. `# zfs create -o mountpoint=/home/<user>/.local/share/containers -o dedup=on zroot/containers`
4. `# zfs allow <user> create,destroy,mount,snapshot zroot/containers`
5. `# chown <user>:<user> /home/<user>/.local/share/containers`
6. `exit`
7. `$ podman system info`


### Describe the results you received

After running `podman system info` (or any Podman command as a regular user):

```
$ podman system info
Error: cannot find root filesystem zroot/containers: exit status 1: "/usr/bin/zfs list -rHp -t filesystem -o name,origin,used,available,mountpoint,compression,type,volsize,quota,referenced,written,logicalused,usedbydataset zroot/containers" => cannot open 'zroot/containers': dataset does not exist
```

### Describe the results you expected

The `zfs` storage driver is able to "see" the dataset and returns the system info without error.

### podman info output

```
# podman --version
podman version 4.7.1
```

```
# uname -a
Linux saturn 6.5.8-arch1-1 containers/podman#1 SMP PREEMPT_DYNAMIC Thu, 19 Oct 2023 22:52:14 +0000 x86_64 GNU/Linux
```

```
# cat /etc/os-release 
NAME="Arch Linux"
PRETTY_NAME="Arch Linux"
ID=arch
BUILD_ID=rolling
ANSI_COLOR="38;2;23;147;209"
HOME_URL="https://archlinux.org/"
DOCUMENTATION_URL="https://wiki.archlinux.org/"
SUPPORT_URL="https://bbs.archlinux.org/"
BUG_REPORT_URL="https://bugs.archlinux.org/"
PRIVACY_POLICY_URL="https://terms.archlinux.org/docs/privacy-policy/"
LOGO=archlinux-logo
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

_No response_

### Additional information

Interestingly, using the `zfs` storage driver as `root` works fine on the same system:
```
# podman system info                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           [63/4503]

  arch: amd64 
  buildahVersion: 1.32.0            
  cgroupControllers:

  cgroupManager: systemd                         
  cgroupVersion: v2
  conmon:            
    package: /usr/bin/conmon is owned by conmon 1:2.1.8-1
    path: /usr/bin/conmon
    version: 'conmon version 2.1.8, commit: 00e08f4a9ca5420de733bf542b930ad58e1a7e7d'
  cpuUtilization:
    idlePercent: 99.98
    systemPercent: 0.01
    userPercent: 0
  cpus: 40 
  databaseBackend: boltdb
  distribution:
    distribution: arch
    version: unknown
  eventLogger: journald
  freeLocks: 2048                         
  hostname: saturn
  idMappings:
    gidmap: null
    uidmap: null
  kernel: 6.5.8-arch1-1
  linkmode: dynamic   
  logDriver: journald
  memFree: 132329963520                 
  memTotal: 135066349568          
  networkBackend: netavark
  networkBackendInfo:
    backend: netavark
    dns:                                    
      package: /usr/lib/podman/aardvark-dns is owned by aardvark-dns 1.8.0-1
      path: /usr/lib/podman/aardvark-dns
      version: aardvark-dns 1.8.0 
    package: /usr/lib/podman/netavark is owned by netavark 1.8.0-1

      +SYSTEMD +SELINUX +APPARMOR +CAP +SECCOMP +EBPF +CRIU +YAJL
```

comments.body: No upstream maintainers work on the zfs, so it is not likely that upstream can help you.  Perhaps people in the community. We recommend everyone use overlayfs on top of whatever file system you have.

> No upstream maintainers work on the zfs, so it is not likely that upstream can help you. Perhaps people in the community. We recommend everyone use overlayfs on top of whatever file system you have.

@rhatdan: Please clarify. Do you mean there are no podman devs who support zfs storage at the moment? Using overlayfs where zfs is native is not desirable.

I came to report the same bug but with a minor variation in the error:

`$ STORAGE_DRIVER=zfs podman system info`
`Error: cannot find root filesystem rpool/var/lib/containers/storage-<user>: exit status 1: "/usr/sbin/zfs fs list -rHp -t filesystem -o name,origin,used,available,mountpoint,compression,type,volsize,quota,referenced,written,logicalused,usedbydataset rpool/var/lib/containers/storage-<user>" => cannot open 'rpool/var/lib/containers/storage-<user>': dataset does not exist`

If the error code is derived from the actual command executed, then it should be noted that: `/usr/sbin/zfs fs list -rHp...` is  not a valid command.

I don't know where to look but I assume someone familiar with the code could verify it isn't a basic fat-finger error with minimal effort.
zfs is not native in the upstream kernel and none of the core development team use it.
I see. How did the current level of support develop? Is the current level of support going to be deprecated?

Since this affects the basic functionality and anything ZFS related is "can't fix/won't fix" some note should be made in the docs so that for those of us who consider this a critical function won't waste our time.
This is a community project, if community wants to support it, then they need to step up.  The volunteers or people who are paid to work on this stuff do not work on zfs.

Understood and agreed. Unfortunately I posess neither the required skills or bandwidth to fix.

As it stands, rootless ZFS support is non-functional. This should be documented somewhere so that users are aware. The volunteers or people who are paid to work on this stuff *do* have access to update the docs. It isn't a big ask to make a footnote in the appropriate location. Maybe an additonal note that devs with ZFS experience would be especially welcome...

podman is running the wrong command:

```
$ podman system reset -f
Error: cannot find root filesystem rpool/containers_user: exit status 1: "/usr/sbin/zfs fs list -rHp -t filesystem -o name,origin,used,available,mountpoint,compression,type,volsize,quota,referenced,written,logicalused,usedbydataset rpool/containers_user" => cannot open 'rpool/containers_user': dataset does not exist
```

The correct command is: `zfs list -Hp -t filesystem -o name,origin,used,available,mountpoint,compression,type,volsize,quota,referenced,written,logicalused,usedbydataset rpool/containers_user`

This was on Debian 12's 4.3.1 though.
Interested in opening a PR?
This code comes from vendor/github.com/mistifyio/go-zfs/v3/

> Interested in opening a PR?

Figuring out first how Ubuntu 24.04 makes podman work with ZFS as backing storage with overlay as driver.  The bug was in Debian 12's podman 4.3.1 though.
I believe this bug is no longer valid on latest podman.  Podman even works on FreeBSD which also uses ZFS.

> I believe this bug is no longer valid on latest podman. Podman even works on FreeBSD which also uses ZFS.

This is good news. Unfortunately all of our relavent systems are running Debian 12. Any idea in what version this was fixed?

> > I believe this bug is no longer valid on latest podman. Podman even works on FreeBSD which also uses ZFS.
> 
> This is good news. Unfortunately all of our relavent systems are running Debian 12. Any idea in what version this was fixed?

Maybe I'm wrong.  Ubuntu 24.04 has podman 4.9.3 working with ZFS as backing filesystem, not as graph driver.  I don't know if it uses fuse-overlayfs to get it working because trying the same without FUSE in Debian 12 fails.

FreeBSD does use podman 4.8.3 with ZFS as graph driver.  Only runs as root, though.

I prefer to use VFS as graph driver over fuse-overlayfs with ZFS as backing filesystem.  This is my `/etc/containers/storage.conf`:

```
[storage]
driver = "vfs"
graphroot = "/var/lib/containers/storage"
runroot = "/run/containers/storage"
```

You have to remove root and user storage directories and run `podman system reset -f`.  You know the drill.
Does Docker work with zfs when run in rootless mode? Or are you running with rootful Docker?

> Does Docker work with zfs when run in rootless mode? Or are you running with rootful Docker?

Haven't tried rootless Docker with ZFS.
That is the issue, you drop Podman because zfs does not work with rootless mode, but then switch to Docker in rootful mode.

> Does Docker work with zfs when run in rootless mode? Or are you running with rootful Docker?

It appears not, which then brings us right back to podman...so we will not be migrating and will have to weigh the benefits of having rootless containers vs the performance and scaling implications of using vfs or overlayfs riding on zfs.

I'll keep an eye on this issue in hopes someone with ZFS chops takes interest and makes our dreams come true.

This is meant to share what I learned so far in trying to get zfs driver to work rootless as I have not found much useful information. I have not got it to fully work yet, but it might help someone else get this over the line.

### Background
Podman runs rootless operations in dedicated user namespace.  By default that namespace will not have access to see any zfs datasets. 

ZFS  added ability for containers/userns to be granted access to work with datasets.   Commit was https://github.com/openzfs/zfs/commit/4ed5e25074ffec266df38556d9b3a928c5e0dee9.

### Steps to get around lack of necessary permissions
To grant permission requires the user namespace to exist. So you need to run a podman command so it will spawn process that will own the rootless usens. E.g. `podman info` . 

This will record the pid of the process in `$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/libpod/tmp/pause.pid`

Now you can give rootless podman access to dataset you want zfs driver to use:

As root  run ``zfs zone /proc/`cat $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/libpod/tmp/pause.pid`/ns/user mypool/container/myuser`` - $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR need to be value from the non-root user

As a test command to create test volume now succeeds: `podman volume create test2`

### Unsolved problems

* Requires root to grant permissions. So podman would not be able to auto setup rootless userns as needed. I could now get incantation of `zfs allow ...`  to delegate necessary access for running `zfs zone` without `root`
* On `system reset` podman tries to unlink root of storage `zfspool/container/user/` (in my case) and fails
* The way zfs driver tried to control mount point is not allowed so running container still fails

`podman run -dt -p 8080:80/tcp docker.io/library/httpd`

```
Error: copying system image from manifest list: writing blob: adding layer with blob "sha256:af302e5c37e9dc1dbe2eadc8f5059d82a914066b541b0d1a6daa91d0cc55057d"/""/"sha256:f5fe472da25334617e6e6467c7ebce41e0ae5580e5bd0ecbf0d573bacd560ecb": creating read-only layer with ID "f5fe472da25334617e6e6467c7ebce41e0ae5580e5bd0ecbf0d573bacd560ecb": exit status 1: "/usr/bin/zfs fs create -o mountpoint=legacy zfspool/container/user/f5fe472da25334617e6e6467c7ebce41e0ae5580e5bd0ecbf0d573bacd560ecb" => cannot create 'zfspool/container/user/f5fe472da25334617e6e6467c7ebce41e0ae5580e5bd0ecbf0d573bacd560ecb': permission denied
```

thank you @vmpn it sorted my setup for rootless zfs storage driver.
I've to run  below script on each reboot to have the zfs loaded for my `rpodman` rootless user.
Also did set zoned=on in the corresponding dataset, due to some reason its missing from setup.

#!/bin/bash
sudo -i -u rpodman -- podman info
zfs set zoned=on rpool/containers
ID=`id -u rpodman`
PID=$(cat /run/user/$ID/libpod/tmp/pause.pid)
zfs zone /proc/$PID/ns/user rpool/containers

@vmpn 

```
$ podman info | grep -E 'driver|graphRoot'
Error: configure storage: cannot find root filesystem zroot/podman: exit status 1: "/usr/bin/zfs list -rHp -t filesystem -o name,origin,used,available,mountpoint,compression,type,volsize,quota,referenced,written,logicalused,usedbydataset zroot/podman" => cannot open 'zroot/podman': dataset does not exist
```

```
$ sudo zfs zone /proc/`cat $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/libpod/tmp/pause.pid`/ns/user zroot/podman
```

Unfortunately by the looks of `volumePath: /home/niko/.local/share/containers/storage/volumes` it seems it doesn't use the `zfs` backend. Any clue why?

Upon closer inspection it looks like it's indeed using the zfs backend but there are still some permission issues:

```
Error: unable to copy from source docker://mongo:8.0: copying system image from manifest list: writing blob: adding layer with blob "sha256:4b3ffd8ccb5201a0fc03585952effb4ed2d1ea5e704d2e7330212fb8b16c86a3"/""/"sha256:073ec47a8c22dcaa4d6e5758799ccefe2f9bde943685830b1bf6fd2395f5eabc": creating read-only layer with ID "073ec47a8c22dcaa4d6e5758799ccefe2f9bde943685830b1bf6fd2395f5eabc": exit status 1: "/usr/bin/zfs create -o mountpoint=legacy zroot/podman/073ec47a8c22dcaa4d6e5758799ccefe2f9bde943685830b1bf6fd2395f5eabc" => cannot create 'zroot/podman/073ec47a8c22dcaa4d6e5758799ccefe2f9bde943685830b1bf6fd2395f5eabc': permission denied
```

I've tried the following with little success:

```
# zfs allow -u niko create,mount,destroy,snapshot zroot/podman
```
It looks like I was missing `zfs set zoned=on zroot/podman`

Thank you for confirming this @darkbasic! 

I aim to setup up a new system and test this over the next few weeks to confirm it is indeed fixed so we can close the original issue.

Got it working on a new Archlinux zfs-rootfs host today thanks to this thread. Below are my notes for a headless host but they also worked for my workstation which I log into graphically (just without `loginctl enable-linger ${me}` for that one)

TL;DR at end

The headless host's zpool and layout:

```
some-host                                  5.82G   186G    96K  none   # Legacy mountpoints if any
some-host/root                             2.40G   186G  2.33G  legacy # (/)
some-host/root/home                        69.1M   186G    96K  legacy # (/home)
some-host/root/home/theUser                69.0M   186G  68.9M  legacy # (/home/theUser)
some-host/root/home/theUser/containers     96K     186G    96K  legacy # (Not mounted anywhere, doesn't need to be)
```

theUser's `~/.config/containers/storage.conf` contents:
```
[storage]
driver = "zfs"

[storage.options.zfs]
fsname = "some-host/root/home/theUser/containers"
mountopt = "nodev"
#size=""
```

If the underprivileged user account isn't going to be logged in (Or if you're using `su -` to become that user which also doesn't make a login session) you will need to persist one with:

`loginctl enable-linger ${theUser}`

Also enable the podman user service as that user `systemctl --user enable --now podman` so the `catatonit` process starts. The one whose namespace we need to pin a zfs dataset to.

After enabling the above two things for theUser (Or if its currently logged into a tty or graphically) you can pgrep the catatonit process directly with `pgrep catatonit` while zoning the dataset. Or using the original XDG method if you need to target more than one user account per machine by reading the pid from `/run/user/$(id -u theUser)/libpod/tmp/pause.pid`

`zfs zone /proc/$(pgrep catatonit)/ns/user some-host/root/home/theUser/containers`

Grant theUser some dataset control it will need to store pulled images and container volumes:

`zfs allow theUser create,destroy,mount,snapshot some-host/root/home/theUser/containers`

Then actually setting the dataset as zoned (Won't work without this):

`zfs set zoned=on some-host/root/home/theUser/containers`

After all this, I was able to create containers on my graphical workstation as my own user, and on this new headless server as its underprivileged user *after* setting `loginctl enable-linger ${theUser}` so it has a persistently logged in session, which spawns the `catatonit` container init process for granting. Creating a new container spawns a new dataset underneath the configured one from storage.conf.


------------------------


A condensed shitty rundown/script:

```
#!/bin/bash
# Sat Nov 15 17:14:47 AEDT 2025
      headless=no
      #headless=yes # Uncomment if the underprivileged container-running user won't be logged in
    targetUser=someUser
 targetDataset=someZpool/someDataset/containers
targetUserHome="$(getent passwd ${targetUser} | cut -d: -f6)"

mv -v ${targetUserHome}/.config/containers/storage.conf ${targetUserHome}/.config/containers/storage.conf.bak.$(date +%s) 2>/dev/null
su - ${targetUser} -c "mkdir -p ${targetUserHome}/.config/containers"
echo -e "[storage]\ndriver = \"zfs\"\n\n[storage.options.zfs]\nfsname = \"${targetDataset}\"\nmountopt = \"nodev\"\n#size=\"\"" | su - ${targetUser} -c "tee -a ${targetUserHome}/.config/containers/storage.conf"
chown $(id -u ${targetUser}):$(id -g ${targetUser}) -v ${targetUserHome}/.config ${targetUserHome}/.config/containers/ ${targetUserHome}/.config/containers/storage.conf
[[ "${headless:-no}" == yes ]] && loginctl enable-linger ${targetUser}
zfs zone /proc/$(</run/user/$(id -u ${targetUser})/libpod/tmp/pause.pid)/ns/user ${targetDataset}
zfs allow ${targetUser} create,destroy,mount,snapshot ${targetDataset}
zfs set zoned=on ${targetDataset}
# The user can now pull images and run containers which will create datasets under ${targetDataset}
```

For persistence I also gave my headless user this permission in /etc/sudoers (via `visudo`) to manage their own container dataset's namespace:

`someUser ALL=(ALL:ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/zfs zone /proc* someZpool/someDataset/containers`

And created/enabled this userspace systemd service: `~/.local/share/systemd/user/zfs_zone_tweak.service`

```
[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=bash -c " while ! pgrep catatonit ; do sleep 1 ; done ; /usr/bin/sudo zfs zone /proc/$(</run/user/$(id -u)/libpod/tmp/pause.pid)/ns/user someZpool/someDataset/containers"

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
```

Then as that user making sure XDG_RUNTIME_DIR was set, enabled it:
`su - someUser -c 'XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/run/user/$(id -u) systemctl enable --user --now zfs_zone_tweak'`

(There are other locations this service file can live for user contexts if this will be for multiple accounts)
Working towards this: https://github.com/openzfs/zfs/pull/18167
